codeSnapshot.label: batch_norm.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

# Batch Normalisation in a CNN
model = nn.Sequential(
    nn.Conv2d(3, 64, 3, padding=1),
    nn.BatchNorm2d(64),   # after conv, before activation
    nn.ReLU(),
    nn.Conv2d(64, 128, 3, padding=1),
    nn.BatchNorm2d(128),
    nn.ReLU()
)

# Manual BatchNorm (1D for clarity)
def batch_norm_manual(x, gamma, beta, eps=1e-5):
    # x: (B, D)
    mu = x.mean(dim=0)            # (D,) — mean across batch
    var = x.var(dim=0, unbiased=False)  # (D,) — variance across batch
    x_hat = (x - mu) / (var + eps).sqrt()  # normalise
    return gamma * x_hat + beta   # scale and shift

B, D = 32, 256
x = torch.randn(B, D)
gamma = torch.ones(D)   # learnable scale, init=1
beta = torch.zeros(D)   # learnable shift, init=0
x_out = batch_norm_manual(x, gamma, beta)
print(f"After BN: mean={x_out.mean():.4f}, std={x_out.std():.4f}")  # ≈ 0, 1

# At inference: use running statistics (not batch stats)
# PyTorch BN tracks: running_mean, running_var
# running_mean = (1-momentum)*running_mean + momentum*batch_mean  (momentum=0.1)
bn = nn.BatchNorm1d(D)
bn.train()  # uses batch statistics
bn.eval()   # uses running_mean, running_var

# Batch size effect
# Large B: stable statistics (BN works well)
# Small B (B=1,2): unstable statistics — use LayerNorm or GroupNorm

eli3: Batch Normalisation (BatchNorm) is a technique that normalises the inputs to each layer so they always have approximately zero mean and unit variance, within each mini-batch. This keeps the input distribution to each layer stable throughout training, so each layer doesn't have to constantly readjust to shifts in its input.

Without BatchNorm, as the weights in early layers change, the distribution of inputs to later layers shifts constantly — called 'internal covariate shift'. This forces later layers to keep re-learning how to handle different input distributions, slowing training. BatchNorm removes this problem.

BatchNorm has two learnable parameters per unit: γ (scale) and β (shift). After normalising to zero mean and unit variance, these learned parameters let the network re-scale if needed. So BatchNorm doesn't force the network to be normalised — it just provides a stable normalised starting point that it can adapt from.

whenWhy.title: Training deep CNNs (ResNet, VGG, EfficientNet)
whenWhy.body: BatchNorm is the standard regularisation technique for convolutional networks. It allows training with higher learning rates (normalised activations prevent gradient explosion), reduces sensitivity to weight initialisation, and acts as a regulariser (mini-batch statistics add noise similar to dropout). ResNet-50 without BN trains poorly; with BN it achieves 76% ImageNet top-1.

whenWhy.title: Stabilising training with high learning rates
whenWhy.body: Ioffe & Szegedy (2015) showed BatchNorm allows 14× higher learning rates without divergence. Normalised inputs limit the effective gradient scale — even if weights in a layer are very large or small, the normalised output has controlled magnitude. This makes the loss landscape smoother and allows larger, faster gradient steps.

whenWhy.title: When BatchNorm fails: small batches, RNNs, or online learning
whenWhy.body: BatchNorm's statistics are unstable with batch size below 8 (high variance estimate). Alternative: LayerNorm (normalise over features, not batch — works for batch size 1) for transformers and RNNs; GroupNorm (normalise over channel groups) for object detection where batch size varies; InstanceNorm (normalise per sample per channel) for style transfer.

deepDiveIntro: BatchNorm (Ioffe & Szegedy, 2015): given a mini-batch of B samples, for each feature dimension, compute the batch mean and variance, subtract the mean and divide by the standard deviation (plus a small epsilon to avoid division by zero), then apply learned scale and shift parameters gamma and beta. Parameters gamma and beta are learned via gradient descent along with the rest of the network. At training time, batch statistics are used. At inference time, exponential moving averages of training statistics are used instead. [1]

The gradient of BatchNorm has a peculiar structure: the gradient with respect to any single input depends on all other samples in the batch, because they all share the same mean and variance. This mini-batch coupling is both BatchNorm's strength (it provides a smoothing regularisation effect) and its limitation (it depends on batch size and is incompatible with sequential models where the batch dimension is meaningful). [1]

deepDive.heading: Forward Pass Algorithm
deepDive.body: Given a mini-batch, compute the mean across all samples for each feature. Compute the variance similarly. Subtract the mean and divide by the square root of variance plus epsilon (a small constant, typically 1e-5, to prevent division by zero). Then apply the learned scale gamma (initialised to 1) and shift beta (initialised to 0). The normalisation is applied per-feature for fully connected layers, or per-channel (averaging over batch and spatial dimensions) for convolutional layers. [1]
deepDive.formula: x̂ = (x − μ_B) / √(σ²_B + ε);  y = γx̂ + β

deepDive.heading: Effect on Gradient Flow
deepDive.body: Normalisation makes the gradient with respect to pre-normalisation activations scale-invariant: if you scale all weights in a layer by a constant, the output after BatchNorm is unchanged because the constant cancels in the normalisation. The gradient through BatchNorm is also modified so that gradients from BatchNorm-normalised layers effectively have adaptive learning rates. This is why BatchNorm allows higher learning rates — the actual gradient signal is self-normalising. [1]

deepDive.heading: Batch Norm in Inference Mode
deepDive.body: At inference time (model.eval() in PyTorch), BatchNorm uses running mean and running variance computed during training instead of the current batch's statistics. These running statistics are updated during training as an exponential moving average of each batch's mean and variance (PyTorch default momentum is 0.1, so the running mean tracks with roughly a 10-step lag). Forgetting to call model.eval() before inference is a common bug: predictions will use noisy statistics from a single sample, producing unstable outputs. [1]

deepDive.heading: BatchNorm vs LayerNorm vs GroupNorm
deepDive.body: The key difference is which dimension you normalise over. BatchNorm normalises over the batch dimension — it needs a reasonably large batch to estimate stable statistics. LayerNorm normalises over the feature dimension, so it works even with a single sample (batch size 1) and is the standard for transformers and NLP. GroupNorm divides channels into groups and normalises within each group — used for object detection where batch size is often 2 due to GPU memory constraints. InstanceNorm normalises per sample per channel and is used for style transfer tasks where per-image normalisation is natural. [2]

takeaways: Normalise mini-batch to μ=0, σ²=1; then learn γ, β to re-scale: y=γ((x−μ)/√(σ²+ε))+β. [1]
Training: batch statistics; inference: running mean/var (call model.eval()!). [1]
Allows 14× higher learning rates; reduces init sensitivity; implicit regularisation. [1]
Fails for small batch sizes (B<8) or sequential models — use LayerNorm instead. [2]
GroupNorm for object detection; InstanceNorm for style transfer. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why does BatchNorm act as a regulariser?
edgeCases.a: BatchNorm's statistics (mean and variance) depend on the random mini-batch composition. Different batches produce different normalisation statistics for each sample — the same sample receives slightly different normalisation depending on which other samples are in its batch. This is random noise analogous to dropout: each sample experiences a slightly different transformation during training. At test time, fixed running statistics remove this noise. This mini-batch dependence is a stochastic regularisation that reduces overfitting — empirically, models trained with BN often need less dropout.

edgeCases.q: What goes wrong when you use BatchNorm with very small batch sizes?
edgeCases.a: With batch size 1 (single sample), the sample's own statistics (mean and variance computed from d features) are used. Variance estimation with one sample has very high variance itself — you're estimating population statistics from a single observation. With batch size 2, the variance estimate is still poor and systematically underestimates the true variance, causing over-normalisation. For detection models where batch size 2 is common due to GPU memory, GroupNorm (with 32 groups, normalising within groups of channels) is used instead.

sources.label: Ioffe & Szegedy (2015) — Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training arXiv:1502.03167
